title: The Ocean Walker

jokes.text: Michael announces he's getting married; parents respond 'Who the hell to?' / 'Who do you think? Rita.' / 'Her?' / 'Why?'
jokes.setup: Michael excitedly announces he's getting married, expecting celebration.
jokes.punchline: Parents' staccato dismissal — 'Her?' / 'Why?' — delivered with utter contempt, no ceremony whatsoever.
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire dismissal is perfectly Bluth — zero parental warmth, maximum self-interest. 'Her?' / 'Why?' lands because it's economical contempt. Character-specific to the Bluth parents' dynamic with Michael.

jokes.text: Michael: 'She needs a green card to stay in the country.' George: 'There it is.' Michael: 'She needs a green card to stay in the country, BECAUSE, secondly...'
jokes.setup: Michael starts to explain why he's marrying Rita by leading with the green card reason, then backtracks to add the love reason.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s 'There it is' perfectly punctuates Michael's accidental self-indictment, and Michael's flustered pivot only makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural comedy. Michael buries himself with his own word order, George's 'There it is' is perfectly timed, and then Michael's desperate attempt to re-sequence the reasons only confirms everyone's suspicion. The structure is almost musical. 'There it is' is highly quotable shorthand for catching someone out. Deserves high score.

jokes.text: Rita's conversation about building houses: 'Instead of making houses, maybe you should make land. On the ocean.'
jokes.setup: Someone explains that finding land is the hardest part of making houses.
jokes.punchline: Rita sincerely suggests making land on the ocean — an idea that sounds profound but reveals she has no concept of geography or physics.
jokes.explanation: This joke is doing triple work: it's funny as a non-sequitur, it establishes Rita's disability through a perfectly constructed exchange, and it also inadvertently foreshadows 'Bluthton' — the ocean development. Beautifully integrated into the episode's plot and theme.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'For Rita had only mentally progressed to the level of a first grader. Michael might have noticed her disability if it had not been masked by her English accent.'
jokes.setup: The show has been dropping hints about Rita's unusual statements.
jokes.punchline: Narrator delivers the full reveal as flat exposition, with the added kicker that an English accent reads as intelligence to Americans.
jokes.explanation: The English-accent-as-intelligence-mask joke is a sharp cultural observation delivered with perfect AD deadpan. It's also the big reveal of the episode's central premise, so it carries enormous narrative weight. The writing is confident enough to just STATE the joke as fact, which is funnier than any setup could be. Highly quotable cultural observation that earns its score.

jokes.text: George Sr. confuses his mistress story: 'Just because a woman gets pregnant doesn't mean you have to marry her... some cheap waitress at a HoJo said she used an IUD.' Lucille: 'It was Stuckey's.' George: 'But I believed you.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. gives Michael unsolicited marriage advice involving a pregnancy and a waitress.
jokes.punchline: Lucille corrects the restaurant, implying she was the waitress — and George's 'But I believed you' reveals he was fooled about the IUD, making Lucille the scheming party in their own origin story.
jokes.explanation: A three-layer joke: George's generic bad advice → Lucille's correction revealing she's the waitress → George's admission she lied about birth control to trap him. Each line escalates the revelation. It's entirely character-specific — only the Bluths have this history — and it recontextualizes their entire marriage in two lines. Outstanding craft.

jokes.text: Michael says they haven't slept together; Lucille says 'This is funny!' and George says 'He's gay!'
jokes.setup: Michael defends his relationship by saying they haven't been physical yet.
jokes.punchline: His parents immediately leap to 'He's gay!' — a recurring Bluth reflex that reveals more about their assumptions than about Michael.
jokes.explanation: Functional Bluth-parent comedy — their reflexive assumptions are always funny — but 'he's gay' as a punchline is well-worn territory for the show by season 3. Gets a solid laugh but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Sr. holds up his hook-less arm: 'Where's your hand?' / 'Oh. I'm having my high school ring put on it, and my hook is stuck in the staircar.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is gesticulating and someone notices his arm is missing its hook.
jokes.punchline: Mundane explanation that is completely insane: high school ring + hook stuck in the staircar. The staircar is itself a running gag.
jokes.explanation: Two separate running gags collide in one throwaway explanation. The joke rewards show knowledge and the casual delivery of 'my hook is stuck in the staircar' as if it's perfectly normal is peak AD absurdism.

jokes.text: Buster sings 'Domo arigato, Mister Roboto' while his hook-less arm is stuck in the staircar.
jokes.setup: Buster's hook is stuck in the staircar.
jokes.punchline: He serenades the moment with 'Mr. Roboto' — a song about a robot, implying he identifies with his mechanical appendage.
jokes.explanation: Buster singing 'Mr. Roboto' in relation to his robot-like hook arm is a perfectly weird piece of character comedy. Only Buster would do this. The visual of it — implied from context — is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Rita: 'I like to keep the light on. That way I can see if you have a monster.'
jokes.setup: Michael is awkwardly explaining why they haven't been physical — he 'gave her the bedroom' to avoid pressure. Rita chimes in with her reason for wanting the light on.
jokes.punchline: 'That way I can see if you have a monster' — ostensibly childlike fear of monsters under the bed, but in context a hilariously innocent misunderstanding of adult bedroom dynamics.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Rita's statement works on two completely different registers simultaneously: innocent child afraid of monsters, and adult sexual innuendo about male anatomy. The writers thread this needle perfectly — it's not explicit enough to be crude but unmistakably double-meaning. The economy is superb (one line does everything), and it's recalled later in the episode, demonstrating its structural importance. Fully earned.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Let's just all relax, and remind ourselves that we don't have any money.' George Sr.: 'She's already gotten our money!'
jokes.setup: Lucille is alarmed that Rita is after the family money. Michael tries to defuse by noting they have no money.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. pivots immediately to 'She's already gotten our money!' — the family's pathological inability to accept their financial reality.
jokes.explanation: Solid running-gag execution — the Bluths' refusal to accept their poverty is always funny, and George Sr.'s immediate leap to 'She's already gotten our money' is a good beat. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Oh, big deal, I had that same basic idea years ago.' — claiming credit for the ocean-top town idea.
jokes.setup: Lucille credits Rita with inspiring 'Bluthton,' the ocean-top town concept. George Sr. immediately disputes it.
jokes.punchline: George claims he had 'that same basic idea years ago' — a man who did not have this idea claiming retroactive genius.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s reflexive claim to credit for everything is a reliable character beat. Competent but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'People just see what they want to see. If she loved you, she'd show it.' — Narrator: 'Michael was worried — his mother may have been making sense.'
jokes.setup: Lucille gives Michael what sounds like genuine maternal wisdom about Rita.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's observation that Michael is worried because 'his mother may have been making sense' — the horror of Lucille accidentally dispensing good advice.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the framing: Lucille making sense is so unusual it's alarming to Michael. The narrator delivers it as genuine concern. Strong character-specific comedy that says something true about the Bluth family dynamic.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'People just see what they want to see. If she loved you, she'd show it.' Narrator: 'Michael was worried — his mother may have been making sense.'
jokes.setup: Lucille gives Michael advice about Rita not showing love.
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes that Michael is alarmed specifically because his mother — reliably wrong about everything — appears to be making a valid point for once.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the meta-layer: Lucille's advice is accidentally correct, which is more alarming to Michael than if she were wrong. The narrator's framing ('his mother may have been making sense') is the punch. Competent but not among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Tobias in a wheelchair/dolly: 'No, no, no, it's a dolly to help videotape your nuptials.' Michael: 'I don't think I need any footage of my nuptials.'
jokes.setup: Michael returns home to find Tobias rolling around in what appears to be a wheelchair.
jokes.punchline: Tobias insists it's a 'dolly' for videotaping — an implausible explanation — and Michael's deadpan 'I don't think I need any footage of my nuptials' implies he understood exactly what Tobias was doing with it.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Tobias in what is obviously a wheelchair being called a 'dolly' is good Tobias comedy. Michael's response implies a double meaning (nuptials/genitals) that he may or may not intend. Solid character work.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Okay, so my legs and my left arm occasionally go to sleep, and you want to call it a health problem.'
jokes.setup: Michael notes that Tobias seems to have had health problems since his hair transplants.
jokes.punchline: Tobias dismisses paralysis-level symptoms — legs AND an arm going to sleep — as not a 'health problem,' delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's pathological denial of his own problems (physical here, usually emotional/relational) is perfectly executed. The specificity of 'legs AND my left arm' going to sleep makes it funnier — it's almost total paralysis but he's offended by the term 'health problem.' Strong Tobias character work.

jokes.text: Rita offers: 'Maybe you should see a bubuman.' Michael: 'A... bubuman?' Rita: 'Oh, uh, a doctor. It's a British expression. Like they say go-up box instead of elevator.'
jokes.setup: Rita helpfully suggests Tobias see a medical professional, using the made-up word 'bubuman.'
jokes.punchline: Her explanation that it's a 'British expression' — alongside the entirely fabricated 'go-up box' for elevator — reveals she's inventing words and Michael accepts it because he assumes British English is exotic.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant execution of the episode's central theme: American credulity about British expressions enables Rita's disability to go undetected. 'Bubuman' and 'go-up box' are inventive nonsense, and Michael's acceptance of them reinforces the English-accent joke from earlier. The joke works on the character level (Rita genuinely doesn't know), the satirical level (Americans defer to British English), and structurally ties back to the narrator's earlier observation.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Oh, like when they say poofter to mean tourist, yes.' Narrator: 'Boy, that Rita's a smarty.'
jokes.setup: Rita gives fake British expressions; Tobias enthusiastically validates with his own wrong example.
jokes.punchline: 'Poofter' does not mean tourist — Tobias confidently confirms nonsense with more nonsense. The narrator's sardonic 'Boy, that Rita's a smarty' is perfectly placed.
jokes.explanation: The joke layers beautifully: Rita invents fake Britishisms, Tobias confirms them with a slur he doesn't know is a slur, and the narrator's sarcasm lands with perfect timing. The audience knows 'poofter' is not a tourist — Tobias's confident wrongness is deliciously specific to his character. The narrator button is superb.

jokes.text: Maeby's screenplay dilemma: 'He's in L.A., she's in Japan. How do I get these two characters together?' Rita: 'Maybe they could walk.' Maeby: 'Across the ocean?' Rita: 'If it's not too deep.'
jokes.setup: Maeby, secretly a film executive, is stuck on how to connect two characters across an ocean.
jokes.punchline: Rita's solution — they walk — is delivered with complete sincerity. Maeby's clarifying 'Across the ocean?' and Rita's 'If it's not too deep' escalates the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: Rita's suggestion to walk across the ocean is the episode's central absurdist premise in miniature — and it ends up naming the episode and Maeby's fake film. The escalation from 'walk' to 'across the ocean?' to 'if it's not too deep' is perfectly paced, and the joke is entirely character-driven (only Rita would say this sincerely).

jokes.text: Maeby: 'No, deep is good. People are going to say, What the hell just happened? I better say I like it. Cause nobody wants to seem stupid.' Rita: 'I like it.' The Ocean Walker.
jokes.setup: Maeby is spinning Rita's absurd walking-across-the-ocean idea into a prestige film pitch.
jokes.punchline: Maeby's theory that audiences say they like things they don't understand to seem smart — and then Rita, who genuinely doesn't understand, immediately says 'I like it' — is meta-textual comedy about art and pretension.
jokes.explanation: A layered meta-joke: Maeby articulates the emperor's-new-clothes logic of prestige film appreciation, and Rita — who can't understand anything — is the perfect validator. The joke works as satire of Hollywood, as character comedy for both Maeby and Rita, and as epistemically ironic commentary (the person who 'gets it' most genuinely is the one who can't understand anything). High craft.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Holy crap, that's going to look good on a hat.'
jokes.setup: Rita comes up with the title 'The Ocean Walker.'
jokes.punchline: Maeby's immediate pivot to merchandise — 'that's going to look good on a hat' — is peak cynical Hollywood producer logic from a teenager.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. Maeby's Hollywood cynicism is always funny, and the specificity of 'on a hat' (not a poster, not a trailer — a hat) is a great detail. The line is also quotable outside the show's context.

jokes.text: Gob immediately assumes Michael wants a magic show at the wedding, talks over Michael's attempt to clarify, and declares 'You want me to do a magic show at the wedding. I'm on it.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob for a favor without finishing his sentence.
jokes.punchline: Gob fills in the blank with 'magic show' before Michael can say anything, and then congratulates himself on reading the room: 'So glad we pushed through that.'
jokes.explanation: 'So glad we pushed through that' is an excellent button to Gob's solipsism — he congratulates both of them for a conversation that only he had. The joke is structurally clean and completely character-specific.

jokes.text: Gob's magic show flashback: fire at previous wedding. 'Put her out! Put her out! Tracy! Thank God we already did the pictures.'
jokes.setup: Michael says 'no magic' referencing a 'one little screw up last time.'
jokes.punchline: Cut to the specific screw-up: Gob set a bride on fire. The kicker — 'Thank God we already did the pictures' — reframes disaster as logistical inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: The name 'Tracy' lands hard — it appears to be Michael's deceased wife, meaning Gob set Michael's first wife on fire at their wedding. The 'Thank God we already did the pictures' line is extraordinarily dark and funny. The economy is superb — enormous backstory payload in one short exchange. Deserves a high score.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Which reminds me that Rita shouldn't wear any hair spray at this thing.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just recalled setting Tracy on fire at a wedding.
jokes.punchline: His practical advice for Rita — no hairspray — is the most understated possible response to having set the last bride ablaze.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to the Tracy-on-fire joke. Gob's only takeaway from setting a bride on fire is the practical advice about hairspray — not remorse, not a promise never to use fire again, just product guidance. Maximum economy, maximum character specificity. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Gob interprets 'Best Man' duty as: 'I am going to get you the single healthiest call girl that this town has ever seen.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains that all Gob needs to do as 'witness' is sign the license.
jokes.punchline: Gob has already committed to providing a prostitute, with the specific qualifier 'healthiest' — as if that's the main consideration when procuring sex work as a wedding gift.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'healthiest' is what elevates this — Gob has thought about this enough to prioritize STI status as the key metric. Perfectly Gob.

jokes.text: Michael: 'This is why I'm calling it a Witness, and not a Best Man.' / 'All you have to do is watch.' Gob: 'Oh, I'm not going to spend this kind of money and not watch.'
jokes.setup: Michael says all Gob has to do is 'watch' (the wedding ceremony).
jokes.punchline: Gob interprets 'watch' as watching Michael with the call girl — 'I'm not going to spend this kind of money and not watch' — two completely different referents colliding.
jokes.explanation: Clean double-meaning joke: Michael and Gob are having entirely different conversations simultaneously. 'I'm not going to spend this kind of money and not watch' is a great line — Gob's logic is perfectly consistent within his own frame. Well-structured.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Rita are wearing their designer jackets inside out. 'Yeah, that way you see the label. Yeah, I mean, that's what you're paying for, right? It's a great statement on fashion.'
jokes.setup: Michael notices they have jackets on inside out.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay spins it as intentional high fashion — 'that's what you're paying for' — when Rita presumably just put her jacket on wrong and Lindsay went along with it.
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of two people in inside-out jackets is strong, and Lindsay's rationalizing it as a fashion statement perfectly captures both characters — Rita's childlike mistake and Lindsay's vanity converting it into 'I meant to do that.' Payoff later in the episode when Lindsay offers to help Rita put her dress on inside out.

jokes.text: Rita: 'Plus, it doesn't tickle your neck.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has made the sophisticated fashion case for wearing labels outward.
jokes.punchline: Rita adds her actual reason — comfort — with total sincerity. The juxtaposition of Lindsay's pretension and Rita's pragmatic childlike logic deflates the whole premise.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button. One line, perfectly timed, completely deflates Lindsay's high-fashion spin by revealing the real and entirely mundane reason. The economy is exceptional — 'it doesn't tickle your neck' does maximum comedic work in five words.

jokes.text: Michael asks Lindsay when she realized she and Tobias had no chance at a physical relationship. Lindsay: 'Oh my God, do you think we have no chance at a physical relationship?' Michael: 'Just now.'
jokes.setup: Michael intends to use Lindsay's relationship insight to help with his own situation.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's alarm reveals she had not reached this conclusion herself — Michael's question inadvertently breaks the news to her. 'Just now' is the perfect deadpan delivery.
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent: Michael assumes shared understanding, Lindsay reveals she had none, and 'Just now' is devastating in its precision. The joke has perfect economy — three exchanges, enormous character revelation, genuine emotional cringe, and a laugh. Michael's timing on 'Just now' is the kind of line that actors make legendary. One of the episode's best-constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Ask her to stay over at a hotel with you tonight. She's going to know what that means.' Michael: 'That is not a bad idea.' Lindsay: 'Don't... don't do that.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay gives Michael advice to invite Rita to a hotel as a signal of romantic intent.
jokes.punchline: Immediately reverses herself — 'Don't... don't do that' — as she processes that she just gave good advice she can't take back.
jokes.explanation: The reversal — giving advice then immediately withdrawing it for unclear reasons — is classic Lindsay self-sabotage. Not the sharpest joke in the episode but functional character comedy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'King... takes his queen... and showers her with diamonds!' — then corrects to 'clubs' and 'club sauce.'
jokes.setup: Gob is rehearsing a magic trick, apparently involving a chess piece concept.
jokes.punchline: The trick goes wrong mid-practice — 'clubs' instead of 'diamonds,' then 'club sauce' — suggesting whatever liquid was meant to look glamorous is actually condiment.
jokes.explanation: The progression from 'diamonds' to 'clubs' to 'club sauce' is a funny deterioration of an already bad magic trick. 'Club sauce' is a great absurdist coinage. Solid Gob material.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I'd hate to be blamed for setting another one of Michael's brides... on fire!'
jokes.setup: Rita asks Gob to do a magic trick.
jokes.punchline: Gob's refusal hinges on 'another one' — confirming the earlier flashback's implication that Gob has a pattern of setting Michael's brides on fire.
jokes.explanation: 'Another one' is doing enormous work — it implies recurrence, it confirms the worst reading of the earlier joke, and Gob's self-awareness that he does this (while still offering to do magic) is perfect. Strong callback that rewards show knowledge.

jokes.text: Rita: 'Oh, lighter fluid — like Uncle Trevor uses! But wherever did it come from?'
jokes.setup: Gob's trick apparently produces lighter fluid.
jokes.punchline: Rita doesn't connect lighter fluid to fire danger; she just associates it with Uncle Trevor (foreshadowing his character) and asks where it came from with wonder, not alarm.
jokes.explanation: Rita's inability to connect lighter fluid to the danger Gob just described (setting brides on fire) is good character comedy. The 'wherever did it come from?' wonder is charming and dark simultaneously.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I just need to find a trick that puts you out of oxygen for 20 minutes.'
jokes.setup: Rita claims she can hold her breath for 20 minutes. Gob wants to use her in an illusion.
jokes.punchline: Gob states his goal — oxygen deprivation for 20 minutes — completely casually, as if this is a normal thing to do to a wedding guest.
jokes.explanation: The casualness of planning oxygen deprivation as entertainment is perfectly Gob. The narrator's interjection that Rita's actual record was 20 seconds (not 20 minutes) adds another layer — Gob is planning to kill her based on an exaggerated claim.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Actually, her record was 20 seconds.'
jokes.setup: Rita claims she can hold her breath for 20 minutes.
jokes.punchline: Narrator flatly corrects: 20 seconds. The gap between claim and reality is enormous — 20 minutes vs. 20 seconds.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's flat correction is a perfect AD move — one clause, enormous comedy. The 20-second actual record makes Gob's planned illusion immediately life-threatening. The deadpan makes it funnier than any elaboration would.

jokes.text: Michael negotiating with Gob: 'Love for her to walk. And not to drown. Or fly through the air. Or catch on fire. Is she allergic to cat fur?' Gob: 'Oop — no tigers.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to pre-emptively veto all of Gob's dangerous ideas.
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of vetoes (drowning, flying, fire) culminates in cat fur/tigers — Gob had tigers ready to go. 'Oop — no tigers' is Gob catching himself before committing a new atrocity.
jokes.explanation: The escalation is beautifully structured — each addition is more extreme than the last, then the cat fur/tigers pivot is unexpected. 'Oop — no tigers' is the perfect Gob response: not 'I wasn't going to use tigers' but an instinctive self-correction that confirms he absolutely was. Great character work.

jokes.text: Michael suggests to Rita that they go to a hotel for 'some alone time.' Rita: 'Maybe our rooms could touch.'
jokes.setup: Michael awkwardly tries to propose a romantic hotel stay to Rita.
jokes.punchline: Rita's response — 'Maybe our rooms could touch' — is a complete misunderstanding of what 'alone time at a hotel' means, replacing the implied sex with rooms being adjacent.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most charming jokes. 'Maybe our rooms could touch' perfectly encapsulates Rita's mental world — she thinks in spatial terms, not relational ones. The innocence is total and the juxtaposition with Michael's entirely different understanding is perfectly constructed. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Rita and Michael negotiating 'sleepover': 'I was thinking maybe we could be in the same room.' / 'Be in the same bed.' / 'Oh I see what you mean, a real sleepover.'
jokes.setup: Michael is slowly, awkwardly escalating toward the sexual implication of a hotel invitation.
jokes.punchline: Rita concludes the entire negotiation by categorizing it as 'a real sleepover' — interpreting adult sex as childhood slumber party. Her genuine excitement seals it.
jokes.explanation: The sequence of escalation — rooms touching → same room → same bed → 'real sleepover' — is well-paced cringe comedy. Rita's interpretation reveals she's excited for the right idea (intimacy) for completely wrong reasons (slumber party). The gap between Michael's increasingly explicit hints and Rita's innocent reframe is the sustained engine of the joke.

jokes.text: Rita: 'Well, maybe I should go home and get my things. I do kind of miss my jammies.' Michael: 'I think they're going to have a lot of that stuff there.' Rita: 'And my tiny teddy.' Michael: 'Good. Yes, get that.'
jokes.setup: Rita wants to bring her sleepover supplies. Michael enthusiastically agrees, assuming 'tiny teddy' means lingerie.
jokes.punchline: Rita means a stuffed animal. Michael's 'Good. Yes, get that' is eager agreement based on a completely wrong assumption.
jokes.explanation: 'Tiny teddy' is a perfect dual-register joke: lingerie brand / stuffed animal. Michael's enthusiastic 'Good. Yes, get that' — completely misreading the situation — is funny in itself and sets up a later payoff when the actual teddy bear is revealed. Excellent misdirection with strong character work.

jokes.text: George Michael driving Rita, who asks to listen to 'young people's music': they end up on 'Hot Potato' (a Wiggles-style children's song). George Michael's growing concern.
jokes.setup: Rita asks for young people's music as a relatable request, suggesting she's hip.
jokes.punchline: The song playing is 'Hot Potato' — unmistakably a children's song. George Michael joins in briefly, then grows visibly concerned.
jokes.explanation: The children's music revelation — on camera, in real time — is the first moment another character directly experiences Rita's mental age and becomes suspicious. The narrator's 'That's when he first grew concerned' is a perfectly dry button. Excellent visual/audio gag.

jokes.text: Trevor to Rita: 'Do you have any idea what a sleepover is? Cause it's not just sleep.' Rita: 'I know what it is, Trevor, I've seen your secret magazine.'
jokes.setup: Trevor tries to warn Rita about what a hotel sleepover with Michael means.
jokes.punchline: Rita claims to know about sex because of Trevor's 'secret magazine' — which is revealed to be a cricket magazine. The implication that Rita equates a cricket magazine with sexual knowledge is perfect characterization.
jokes.explanation: The 'secret magazine' setup implies something adult/pornographic; the cricket magazine payoff (delivered later) reframes it entirely. Rita's confidence that she knows what a sleepover is because of a cricket magazine is a great character beat.

jokes.text: Rita: 'I want to have grown-up fun. Spoon and figgy pudding. Rabbit downdum druggary's hollow. And I want to have pop-pop.' Trevor: 'Well, the very fact that you call it that, tells me you're not ready.'
jokes.setup: Rita insists she's ready for adult intimacy.
jokes.punchline: Her description of sex — 'spoon and figgy pudding,' 'rabbit downdum druggary's hollow,' and 'pop-pop' — is completely invented child-speak. Trevor's response ('the very fact that you call it that') mirrors the earlier Rita/Michael dynamic but from a protective angle.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Spoon and figgy pudding,' 'rabbit downdum druggary's hollow,' and 'pop-pop' are extraordinarily inventive euphemisms for sex — they sound like genuine child-logic applied to something the speaker doesn't fully understand. The joke is original (no other show does this), character-perfect, and genuinely surprising. 'Pop-pop' alone is iconic. Trevor's response — a mirror of the hotel joke — adds structural elegance. Outstanding.

jokes.text: Trevor: 'And for your information, that's a cricket magazine.' — revealing the 'secret magazine' was about cricket all along.
jokes.setup: Rita claimed to know about sex from Trevor's 'secret magazine.'
jokes.punchline: It's a cricket magazine. Trevor's defensiveness about it — 'for your information' — implies he's embarrassed about being passionate about cricket.
jokes.explanation: A tight callback payoff — the 'secret magazine' that sounded seedy is cricket. The joke has a double layer: it's funny that Trevor is defensive about cricket, and it retroactively makes Rita's claim to sexual knowledge even more absurd. Great misdirection payoff.

jokes.text: Trevor: 'I mean, it's not your fault your parents were cousins, but... here we are.'
jokes.setup: Trevor is lecturing Rita about her limitations.
jokes.punchline: He casually attributes her condition to parental consanguinity — delivered with resigned British acceptance: 'but... here we are.'
jokes.explanation: 'But... here we are' is an excellent button — British understatement applied to a deeply dark situation. The joke is casually delivered which makes it darker. Also sets up the later cousin-marriage joke.

jokes.text: Rita: 'Well, Michael will be my cousin soon enough, cause we're getting married.' Trevor and others: 'Married?! Married!' with escalating chaos.
jokes.setup: Rita explains why it's fine for Michael to be her cousin — because they're getting married.
jokes.punchline: Rita doesn't understand that marriage doesn't make someone a cousin; this makes her logic internally consistent but completely wrong, and the chaos it produces in others is perfect.
jokes.explanation: Rita's logic — marriage = cousin — is childlike and internally consistent within her worldview. The reaction of everyone shouting 'Married! Married!' is good escalation comedy. Also connects to the later George Michael cousin-marriage speech.

jokes.text: Trevor miming 'invisible locks' on the door: 'Lock. Lock. Lock.' while Rita protests 'That's not fair!'
jokes.setup: Trevor declares he's putting 'invisible locks' on the door to prevent Rita from leaving.
jokes.punchline: He mimes turning invisible locks while making the sound 'Lock. Lock. Lock.' and Rita is genuinely trapped — later she cannot find the locks to undo them. The absurdism of invisible locks being an actual plot device.
jokes.explanation: The invisible locks are a genuinely original absurdist bit that the show commits to fully — later Rita literally cannot escape because she can't see the locks. Trevor miming 'Lock. Lock. Lock.' with complete authority is a great visual. The show earns this absurdism by making it narratively functional.

jokes.text: Trevor: 'I've got to find a new hiding place for that magazine.'
jokes.setup: Rita revealed that she had seen Trevor's 'secret' cricket magazine, which was supposedly hidden.
jokes.punchline: Trevor's coda — worried about hiding his cricket magazine more securely — treats cricket fandom as shameful, while still being completely mundane.
jokes.explanation: Good button on the cricket magazine gag. The embarrassment of hiding a cricket magazine as if it were pornography has accumulated enough over the scene to be genuinely funny here.

jokes.text: Michael calls his father from the hotel — awkwardly claims Rita is 'in the shower.' George Sr.: 'I'll wait.' Narrator: 'Let's wait.'
jokes.setup: Michael calls his father to share his romantic news, then realizes Rita isn't there.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s 'I'll wait' — happy to hold while Michael is stranded — and the narrator adopting the same 'Let's wait' implies shared audience anticipation of Michael's humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'Let's wait' is a great self-referential touch — the narrator usually just narrates, not participates. George Sr. being cheerfully willing to wait while Michael sweats is good cringe comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr., watching TV from hotel arrest: 'Wow, that Blossom blew up, huh?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is under house arrest at the hotel with nothing to do while waiting for Michael.
jokes.punchline: He's apparently watching a TV movie or tabloid show about 'Blossom' (the 90s sitcom star, Mayim Bialik) and his commentary is completely off-topic from Michael's crisis.
jokes.explanation: The 'Blossom blew up' line is a funny celebrity-aging joke and perfectly captures George Sr.'s self-absorption — he's comfortable watching TV while Michael is humiliated. Gets funnier in retrospect given Mayim Bialik's later career on Big Bang Theory. Rewatch bonus for that context.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And that local calls were $2.95 a minute.' Michael: 'Come on!' Narrator: 'That's where they get you.'
jokes.setup: Michael has accepted Rita isn't coming; narrator notes he also realizes local calls are $2.95.
jokes.punchline: 'That's where they get you' — the narrator expressing consumer frustration about hotel phone rates, breaking from pure narration into editorial. The mundane hotel-pricing grievance next to Michael's romantic disaster.
jokes.explanation: The narrator breaking character to editorialize about hotel phone pricing is a great meta-move. 'That's where they get you' — in the same flat delivery as all the serious narration — perfectly deflates Michael's emotional moment with a mundane grievance. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Rita: 'Trevor locked the door. He doesn't want me to be with you.' Michael: 'What, he locked the door? You can't unlock this door?' Rita: 'I can't even see half the locks! They're invisible. Trevor's the only one who can see them.'
jokes.setup: Michael wants Rita to let him in; she explains she literally can't.
jokes.punchline: The invisible locks from the earlier scene (joke #44) are now a plot obstacle. Rita's 'I can't even see half the locks' and 'Trevor's the only one who can see them' is the full payoff of the absurdist premise.
jokes.explanation: The invisible locks deliver on their absurdist promise. The show fully commits: they are a real plot device, Rita is genuinely trapped, and the explanation to Michael — who has to accept this at face value — is both funny and characterizes both of them perfectly. The escalation from 'invisible locks' as a silly gag to 'invisible locks' as an actual narrative obstacle is impressive.

jokes.text: Michael and Rita escape on... something. Rita cheering 'Yay! Michael! Yay!' as they flee. Then they hit something: 'I hate that (bleep) doll.'
jokes.setup: Michael has climbed up to rescue Rita from her window; they make a dramatic escape.
jokes.punchline: Their escape is halted by colliding with 'that (bleep) doll' — presumably Tobias on his dolly/wheelchair, referenced earlier. The bleep and the frustrated comment puncture the romantic rescue.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Tobias's dolly — appearing at the worst moment to impede a romantic rescue — is a good physical comedy payoff. The bleep suggests a stronger oath that's funnier uncensored. Rita's 'Yay! Michael! Yay!' juxtaposed with the collision is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Michael's grand romantic speech: 'Nothing can stop us now. Nothing in the world of man nor beast, nor time is...' — interrupted by the staircar.
jokes.setup: Michael is building to a heroic proclamation as they escape.
jokes.punchline: He's cut off mid-sentence — presumably by the staircar crashing or blocking them. The staircar as romantic-moment killer.
jokes.explanation: The staircar interrupting Michael's romantic moment is a good escalation-to-deflation. The long windup ('Nothing in the world of man nor beast, nor time is...') makes the interruption funnier.

jokes.text: George Sr., seeing Rita's uncle Trevor arrive: 'Let the hand-outs begin!'
jokes.setup: Trevor arrives to confront the Bluths about Rita marrying Michael.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. assumes Trevor is there for money, shouting 'Let the hand-outs begin!' — which is completely wrong and also ironic given the Bluths have no money.
jokes.explanation: Functional George Sr. character comedy — his reflexive suspicion of everyone's motives and his assumption that money is always the issue is consistent with the character. Not a highlight but gets the job done.

jokes.text: Trevor reveals Rita is mentally challenged AND owns all of Wee Britain. George Sr. and Lucille: 'Millions?' / 'Mentally challenged?' Lucille: 'Shh! It's not polite.'
jokes.setup: Trevor exposes Rita's mental disability to stop the marriage.
jokes.punchline: The Bluths' priorities are instantly revealed: George Sr. fixates on 'Millions?' and Lucille's 'Shh! It's not polite' is delivered after 'mentally challenged' but clearly as social performance, not genuine concern.
jokes.explanation: The Bluths' reaction perfectly exposes their characters: George Sr. only hears 'millions,' Lucille performs propriety while being entirely mercenary. The sequence of reactions — and their order — is the joke.

jokes.text: Trevor: 'Yes, it's funny how Americans don't pick up on that. I think it's cause of her accent and the plastic surgery she's been able to afford.'
jokes.setup: Trevor notes that Americans failed to notice Rita's disability.
jokes.punchline: He identifies the accent (from the narrator's earlier observation) AND adds plastic surgery as a second reason — meaning Rita looks 'normal' because she's rich enough to maintain appearances.
jokes.explanation: The plastic surgery addition to the 'accent' explanation is a sharp satirical point — wealth allows a disabled person to 'pass' as neurotypical. The joke rewards the callback while adding new information.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Oh, Legoland.' George Sr.: 'I think they said they were going to Legoland.' Lucille: 'They did say Legoland.' Trevor: 'I didn't hear anything about Legoland.' Lucille/George Sr.: 'He doesn't know what's talking about. We think he may be mentally challenged.'
jokes.setup: They need to find Michael and Rita; Lucille randomly says 'Legoland.'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. and Lucille immediately back each other's obvious lie, then when Trevor calls it out, they turn the 'mentally challenged' accusation on him — a deflection that mirrors what was just said about Rita.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed. The Bluths instantly deploy 'mentally challenged' as a deflection weapon against the person who just used it — perfect Bluth sociopathy. The Legoland gambit is absurd and the closing 'We think he may be mentally challenged' is a great button. This is quintessential Arrested Development: dark, character-specific, and structurally elegant.

jokes.text: Lucille to Trevor: 'We have got 24 hours to get them married before Michael figures out what she really is.' Trevor: 'This is the first time in two years you've sounded like George Bluth.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is scheming to keep Michael married to Rita for her money, allying with her enemy Trevor.
jokes.punchline: Trevor's backhanded compliment — 'This is the first time in two years you've sounded like George Bluth' — equates Lucille's scheme with George Sr.'s notorious awfulness, while making it a compliment in context.
jokes.explanation: The compliment framing of George Sr.'s sociopathy as an aspirational quality is a strong character joke. The implication that George Sr. would immediately recognize exploiting a disabled person's wealth as an opportunity, and that this is what Lucille has finally done, is darkly funny and character-true.

jokes.text: Rita: 'I want to have sexual relations.' Michael: 'Mere fact that you call it that tells me that you are ready.'
jokes.setup: Rita announces her desire for intimacy; Michael had earlier rejected this phrasing.
jokes.punchline: Michael REVERSES his position entirely — earlier 'the very fact that you call it that tells me you're NOT ready' — now inverted because he wants to sleep with her. He's the same person applying the rule in self-serving ways.
jokes.explanation: Structurally one of the episode's best jokes. It's a perfect inversion of the earlier Trevor/Rita exchange — where Trevor used the 'mere fact' logic to say Rita was NOT ready, now Michael uses the identical logic to declare she IS ready — purely because it suits him. It exposes Michael's self-delusion with a single line. The economy is impeccable.

jokes.text: George Michael appears at the hotel room door unexpectedly. Michael says: 'Um, go ahead and... talk out here.' — massive understatement.
jokes.setup: Michael is in a hotel room with Rita, about to be intimate.
jokes.punchline: George Michael walks in at exactly the wrong moment. Michael's mortified 'Um, go ahead and... talk out here' implies he's shooing his son out before things get worse.
jokes.explanation: The awkward-family-member-intrusion is a reliable comedic beat. Michael's extremely understated management of the situation is funny, though the beat doesn't add much beyond the cringe of the situation.

jokes.text: George Michael arrives having spent the day with Rita: 'We just got back from the bumpaddle courts. Tomorrow, I'm teaching her baseball.' Michael: 'Yeah. That may take a while.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been innocently hanging out with Rita while Michael was waiting at the hotel.
jokes.punchline: 'Bumpaddle courts' is apparently a child's game Rita plays; 'Tomorrow I'm teaching her baseball' suggests Rita needs to be taught children's sports. Michael's 'That may take a while' shows dawning awareness.
jokes.explanation: Michael's 'That may take a while' is doing double duty — acknowledging the difficulty of teaching baseball AND hinting at his growing suspicion about Rita's mental age. Competent understatement comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael's videotape of Rita playing with the plastic fruit bowl — eating a plastic banana, going back for the grapes. Michael and George Michael watching in silent mortification.
jokes.setup: George Michael has been 'videotaping Rita' — Michael initially misunderstands why this would be embarrassing.
jokes.punchline: The tape shows Rita eating plastic fruit with complete delight. Michael's dawning horror as he watches — 'Is she going back in for another?' / 'She really liked those' — is the central reveal of Rita's disability to Michael.
jokes.explanation: The plastic fruit reveal is the episode's dramatic centerpiece and works brilliantly as comedy. The construction is impeccable: Michael's misunderstanding of why the tape is embarrassing, then the actual tape, then his slow 'Oh, no, she's going into the grapes,' then 'She really liked those' — George Michael's flat delivery of that line is devastating. Michael's dawning understanding playing out in real time over fake fruit is both funny and emotionally resonant. One of the episode's best constructed moments.

jokes.text: Michael to George Michael about the plastic fruit: 'It's the plastic fruit, Dad.' Michael: 'It's very convincing fruit, okay?'
jokes.setup: George Michael gently indicates what the tape proves about Rita.
jokes.punchline: Michael's defense of the fruit — 'It's very convincing fruit, okay?' — is an absurd misdirection as he tries to find any explanation other than the obvious one.
jokes.explanation: 'It's very convincing fruit, okay?' is immediately quotable — Michael's desperate logic applied to clearly fake decorative fruit. The joke works as denial comedy and character comedy simultaneously.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Well, you know, they don't even have bananas in England, okay, son?'
jokes.setup: George Michael implies Rita eating plastic bananas indicates something; Michael keeps finding explanations.
jokes.punchline: Michael claims England doesn't have bananas, which is obviously false — and he says it with conviction to his teenage son, who presumably knows better.
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperate escalation of excuses peaks with a factually wrong claim delivered with complete authority: 'they don't even have bananas in England.' The absurdity is perfectly calibrated — it's exactly wrong enough to be funny, and 'okay, son?' is a great tag.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I think she might be... retar... retarded.' Michael: 'I'm sorry. I'm really sorry.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been trying to gently guide his father to the conclusion about Rita.
jokes.punchline: His stumbling over the word — 'retar... retarded' — and Michael's repeated 'I'm sorry' show both characters' genuine discomfort with the situation, making it funny through awkwardness rather than cruelty.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is the mutual embarrassment — George Michael struggling to say it, Michael apologizing reflexively. It's a cringe beat that also functions as character work for both. Not the sharpest joke but earns its place.

jokes.text: Rita appears with a small stuffed bear: 'Say hello to my tiny teddy.' Michael (horrified): 'Oh! Tiny Teddy!'
jokes.setup: Earlier, Michael enthusiastically encouraged Rita to bring her 'tiny teddy' to the hotel.
jokes.punchline: Full visual payoff: it's a small stuffed bear. Michael's horrified 'Oh! Tiny Teddy!' is the sound of a man realizing every assumption he made was wrong.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best payoffs. The misdirection was set up perfectly ('Good. Yes, get that'), and the stuffed bear arriving as the tiny teddy is exactly as absurd and heartbreaking as it needed to be. Michael's cry of 'Oh! Tiny Teddy!' — the recognition that the whole evening is doomed — is earned comedy at its finest. The visual of Michael's face alongside a small stuffed bear completes the episode's central joke about self-delusion.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And that's when it dawned on Michael.' — then plays back: 'Oh, you teach kids, huh?' / 'Oh, I like to think they teach me.' / 'That way I can see if you have a monster.' / 'Be careful.'
jokes.setup: Michael's epiphany plays out as the narrator replays earlier moments in new context.
jokes.punchline: The 'monster' line (from joke #9) and 'Be careful' replay — things that seemed charming earlier now read as childlike red flags. The rewatch of the episode's own footage is a meta-commentary on the audience's own obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: This is Arrested Development operating at its highest level. The show replays its own footage to make a structural point — Michael (and the audience) was given all the evidence and still missed it. The 'monster' line, originally funny as innocent wordplay, is now devastating. This kind of layered, self-referential construction is why the show rewards rewatching. The structure is impeccable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I've made a huge mistake.'
jokes.setup: Michael has realized his fiancée is mentally disabled.
jokes.punchline: 'I've made a huge mistake' — the show's most iconic running gag, deployed here with maximum narrative weight.
jokes.explanation: The line itself is a series-long running gag — impact score is elevated by cumulative weight of all its previous uses. In this specific context it lands with maximum comic and emotional resonance because Michael has earned it through obliviousness. The quotability is a 10 because this line transcended the show. Originality is lower because it's a repeat, but the deployment here is well-timed.

jokes.text: Michael 'tests' Rita: 'How many houses are there in the British Parliament?' Rita: 'Seven.' Michael: 'Are there?' Narrator: 'The answer is three.' Michael: 'Does sound about right.' Rita: 'Three is the correct answer.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to test Rita's intelligence covertly to confirm his suspicions.
jokes.punchline: Rita says 'seven'; the narrator confirms it's three; Michael says 'Does sound about right' — being sycophantic even while testing. Then Rita corrects herself to three but only because Michael coached her. The narrator's correction demolishes both of them.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully layered. The joke operates on three levels: Rita's wrong answer, Michael's sycophantic 'Does sound about right,' and Rita's self-correction that proves only she can parrot back what was just said. Also: the narrator breaking in to correct both of them is a great structural move. 'Three is the correct answer' at the end is Rita demonstrating she heard the word 'three' just said, not that she knows the answer.

jokes.text: Michael uses celibacy as an excuse: 'I think that we should wait until after we're married.' Rita: 'Married! Married, married, married!'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to postpone sex by suggesting they wait until marriage — using 'marriage' as an escape.
jokes.punchline: Rita's ecstatic 'Married! Married, married, married!' echoes the earlier 'Married!' chaos (joke #43) — but now it's pure joy, not alarm. The word triggers opposite reactions in different characters throughout the episode.
jokes.explanation: The running 'Married!' reaction is a nice structural device — the same word producing panic or joy depending on the speaker. Michael using marriage as a sex dodge while trapped with someone who genuinely wants to marry him is good ironic situational comedy.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And Michael felt he deserved a $15 thing of candy beans.'
jokes.setup: Michael has survived an evening of sexual awkwardness with Rita.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's reward for Michael's restraint — hotel candy beans at an absurd price — reduces his moral victory to a vending machine purchase. The specificity of '$15 thing of candy beans' is perfect.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable narrator lines. '$15 thing of candy beans' is specific enough to be absurdist (who calls them 'candy beans'?) while being relatable (hotel snacks are expensive). The narrator treating this as a deserved reward is perfectly calibrated deadpan. The word 'thing' instead of 'bag' or 'box' adds to the perfect off-handedness.

jokes.text: The Bluth parents throw Michael a surprise wedding — 'On us. Everything you see.' 'Show them the waffle bar, Larry.'
jokes.setup: Michael prepared to let Rita down gently; instead he walks into a surprise wedding.
jokes.punchline: 'Show them the waffle bar, Larry' — the specific detail of a waffle bar at a wedding, plus 'Larry' as an unnamed party functionary, captures the Bluths' bizarre, mercenary celebration.
jokes.explanation: The waffle bar is a solid absurdist detail for a rushed surprise wedding. 'Larry' as an unnamed character given instructions creates a funny implied world. Functional comedy that establishes the scene's chaos.

jokes.text: Rita: 'I want to get married and have sexual relations like you promised.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to delay the wedding.
jokes.punchline: Rita holds him to a promise of 'sexual relations' — the clinical phrase in her innocent mouth remains just as funny on the second use, now with contract-enforcement energy.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'sexual relations' in Rita's voice continues to be funny — now she's treating it as a contractual obligation, which adds a new dimension. The 'like you promised' framing is a good addition.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Where did you get this firecracker? I love her, Michael.' Lucille: 'So do I. I want to kiss my new daughter-in-law.' — Then pushes to kiss Rita on the lips. Michael: 'No, no, Dad, please take it easy.'
jokes.setup: Rita has just announced she wants to 'have sexual relations' in front of the parents.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. loves her for this; Lucille wants to kiss her on the lips — both parents' affection is entirely mercenary (they know about her millions) but performed as genuine warmth, which is nauseating.
jokes.explanation: The Bluths performing affection they don't feel for mercenary reasons is classic character work. George Sr.'s 'firecracker' is a great word choice. The lip-kiss attempt by Lucille adds the right level of discomfort.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I'll help you put it on inside out!' — offering to help Rita with her wedding dress the wrong way.
jokes.setup: Everyone is rushing toward the wedding; Lindsay offers to help Rita with her dress.
jokes.punchline: 'I'll help you put it on inside out!' — calling back to the inside-out jacket incident. Lindsay has fully committed to this as their thing.
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical callback that also builds character — Lindsay has fully adopted the inside-out look she originally rationalized. The offer to put the wedding dress on inside out is both funny and darkly appropriate for this entire situation. Great callback payoff.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'The rest will just be little happy surprises along the way. Like you said.' Michael: 'Yeah. With the exception of the retard thing.'
jokes.setup: Lucille echoes Michael's own words back to him about 'little surprises.'
jokes.punchline: Michael's edit — 'Yeah. With the exception of the retard thing' — acknowledges the episode's central revelation using the same casual framing. The word lands hard precisely because Michael is using his own earlier logic against himself.
jokes.explanation: The episode bookends itself perfectly: Michael's own words ('little surprises') return to frame the disclosure of Rita's disability. 'With the exception of the retard thing' is a devastating understatement — and the callback structure makes it feel earned rather than gratuitous. The full-circle construction is excellent writing.

jokes.text: Michael: 'She's rich.' / Lucille: 'This is a new low, even for you, Mom.'
jokes.setup: Lucille admits to liking Rita; Michael cuts to the real reason.
jokes.punchline: 'She's rich' said as the actual reason — and Michael's 'This is a new low, even for you, Mom' implies there are many previous lows to compare against.
jokes.explanation: The 'new low, even for you' construction implies the rich history of Lucille's lows. Functional character comedy with good economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm crazy about the little dullard.' — Lucille's explanation for supporting the marriage.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lucille for knowing Rita's disability and still pushing the wedding.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's explanation — 'What can I say? I'm crazy about the little dullard' — is a masterpiece of Lucille comedy: simultaneously an insult, a term of endearment, and a complete deflection from the real motive (money).
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Little dullard' is a perfect Lucille Bluth coinage — condescending, specific, and delivered with apparent warmth that makes it colder. It's entirely character-specific (only Lucille says this), maximally economical, and highly quotable. The gap between 'I'm crazy about her' and calling her a 'dullard' is the joke, and it lands perfectly.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Larry go to a mirror!' — followed by rapid firing: 'You're fired.' / 'You're fired.' / 'What?!' / 'It's over.'
jokes.setup: The confrontation between Michael and Lucille erupts; a wedding coordinator/staff member named Larry is caught in the middle.
jokes.punchline: 'Larry go to a mirror!' is an inscrutable command during a family meltdown — why does Larry need a mirror right now? Both Michael and Lucille firing each other/Larry in rapid succession, then 'It's over' — complete chaos.
jokes.explanation: 'Larry go to a mirror' is a great absurdist non-sequitur in the midst of family chaos — we never understand why Larry needs a mirror. The rapid-fire mutual firings are chaotic comedy. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael fires the wedding staff mid-ceremony: 'You're fired.' / Lucille immediately echoes: 'You're fired.'
jokes.setup: Michael wants to call off the wedding and dismisses the hired help.
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately mirrors 'You're fired' — whether firing someone else or supporting Michael, her reflex response is the same authoritarian command.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's reflexive 'You're fired' is character-consistent — she fires people habitually. The echo effect is funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael and wedding coordinator: 'And this is how you tell me, in the middle of a wedding?' / 'You shouldn't have said anything.'
jokes.setup: The coordinator is being fired mid-wedding setup.
jokes.punchline: 'You shouldn't have said anything' — the coordinator's advice implies Michael should have quietly gone through with the wedding rather than disrupting the event.
jokes.explanation: Competent situational comedy. The wedding coordinator prioritizing the event over Michael's crisis is a decent joke about professional priorities. Not a highlight.

jokes.text: George Michael's cousin-marriage speech: 'In some states, it's legal to marry your own cousin. California's blocked it twice, but that's only because they tacked it onto an estate law thing that wasn't gonna pass. We had the signatures...'
jokes.setup: George Michael is advising Michael on whether to go through with marrying Rita despite knowing the truth.
jokes.punchline: George Michael — who is in love with his own cousin (Maeby) — has detailed, passionate knowledge of cousin marriage legality. 'We had the signatures' reveals he's been personally involved in a signature campaign to legalize cousin marriage.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's highest-craft moments and arguably the funniest joke in the episode. George Michael's hyper-specific knowledge of cousin marriage legislation — including the California failure and 'we had the signatures' — is only possible if you know his ongoing feelings for Maeby. The line 'we had the signatures' implies he was PERSONALLY CIRCULATING A PETITION to make cousin marriage legal, entirely in the subtext, delivered as matter-of-fact legal analysis. This is Arrested Development at its most densely layered. Outstanding character integration, extraordinary originality, and a joke that becomes funnier the more you know about the show.

jokes.text: Michael: 'There wouldn't be any real intimacy, and it would be the strongest marriage in the family.'
jokes.setup: Michael is weighing the pros and cons of marrying Rita despite knowing she's mentally disabled.
jokes.punchline: 'The strongest marriage in the family' — a compliment that is simultaneously a devastating indictment of every other Bluth marriage.
jokes.explanation: A great line that rewards knowledge of the show. The Bluth family marriages are universally terrible, so a sexless marriage to a mentally disabled person is genuinely the strongest option. The deadpan delivery of this absurd logic is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: Lindsay runs out at the reveal, wearing an inside-out bridesmaid's dress. Michael: 'You better catch her, Dad.' George Sr.: 'That's not gonna be easy. She's an Olympic Silver.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is upset and flees the scene.
jokes.punchline: The inside-out dress is the visual callback payoff; George Sr.'s 'Olympic Silver' refers to Rita — momentarily confusing who they're talking about — while also being a casual, absurd sports reference as an obstacle to chasing someone.
jokes.explanation: Three callbacks in one beat: Lindsay's inside-out dress (now a bridesmaid's dress, fully committed), George Sr. referencing Rita's Olympics status as an obstacle, and the gentle confusion about which woman they're chasing. The economy is excellent. 'That's not gonna be easy. She's an Olympic Silver' is a perfect non-sequitur that makes complete sense in context.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Just figured that one out.' — about Rita being an Olympic Silver.
jokes.setup: Michael mentions Rita is an Olympic Silver medalist.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s 'Just figured that one out' — admitting he had no idea, revealing he's been in the dark about a major fact about his son's fiancée.
jokes.explanation: Solid button — George Sr. was only just processing Rita's athletic history. Not a standout joke but works as a quick beat.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Okay. Well, I'll have to go stop production on The Ocean Walker.'
jokes.setup: The wedding is off; Maeby has already greenlit 'The Ocean Walker' based on Rita's absurd idea.
jokes.punchline: Maeby has to halt a film production — the entire B-plot of her fake movie studio has reached this point — based on the ending of a conversation with a mentally disabled woman who suggested walking across the ocean.
jokes.explanation: The callback that ties the B-plot to the main resolution. 'I'll have to go stop production on The Ocean Walker' is funny because it implies actual production has already started on a movie based on a mentally disabled woman's sincere suggestion to walk across an ocean. Maeby's casual 'Okay, well' is the perfect tone for a teenager who greenlights films she can't finish.

jokes.text: Rita to Michael: 'I didn't know until they told me.' Michael: 'No, I'm just a narcissist who was too self-involved to see the truth.' Rita: 'That makes no sense to me.' Michael: 'Yeah, maybe you're right. Maybe I'm being too hard on myself.'
jokes.setup: Michael self-flagellates about missing Rita's disability. Rita responds.
jokes.punchline: Rita's 'That makes no sense to me' is sincere incomprehension — but Michael hears it as therapeutic pushback, leading him to conclude he was being 'too hard on himself.' He uses Rita's confusion as emotional validation.
jokes.explanation: A deeply satisfying joke that encapsulates the entire episode's theme. Michael is still interpreting Rita's responses through his own projections — her 'that makes no sense' is incomprehension, not wisdom, but Michael takes comfort from it. He hasn't learned anything. The irony is complete and structurally elegant.

jokes.text: Rita: 'And you deserve to find somebody a lot more like you.' Michael: 'I don't think that either of us would be happy.'
jokes.setup: Michael is letting Rita down gently, saying she deserves someone more like her.
jokes.punchline: He immediately walks back his own generous statement — 'I don't think that either of us would be happy' — revealing that 'someone more like you' (i.e., mentally disabled) wouldn't make Michael happy. It's accidentally cruel.
jokes.explanation: Michael's accidental reveal that he wouldn't be happy with someone 'more like' Rita — i.e., mentally disabled — is an inadvertent cruelty that lands as dark comedy. His obliviousness continues to the end.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You're gonna be across an ocean.' Rita: 'Well, you can always walk.' Michael: 'Not on the water.' Rita: 'Oh, Michael, you're such a grown-up.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains visiting Rita will be hard because she's across an ocean.
jokes.punchline: Rita's 'You can always walk' — across the ocean — is the episode title's callback in the goodbye scene. Michael's 'Not on the water' gets Rita's ultimate judgment: 'You're such a grown-up.' Rita calling Michael a 'grown-up' as a slight is the perfect ironic button.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The callback to 'walking across the ocean' in the farewell scene — where Rita sincerely suggests it again and Michael declines — is structurally perfect. Rita's parting judgment that Michael is 'such a grown-up' (for knowing you can't walk on water) is bittersweet, ironic, and deeply funny. She means it as a mild criticism; it is, in her world, the highest compliment. The line ties together the episode's themes of perception, self-limitation, and who we project onto others. Outstanding.

jokes.text: Rita appears to walk on water — or at least, it looks like she does. Michael and Gob watch.
jokes.setup: Rita has left the docks; Michael and Gob watch her go.
jokes.punchline: Rita appears to be walking on the water — the episode's title and central absurdist premise literalized. The visual of her apparently walking on the ocean surface is the episode's biggest visual gag.
jokes.explanation: The episode's climactic visual gag is exceptional. 'The Ocean Walker' goes from absurdist child-logic to apparent reality — Rita seemingly walks on water, which is both the punchline to the episode's entire premise and a genuine wonder. The show earns this moment by spending 20 minutes setting up Rita's perspective on the world. Whether she's on a hidden platform or pier doesn't diminish the visual impact. This is Arrested Development's gift: making its most absurdist moments feel inevitable and earned. Near the top of the episode's comedy craft.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Hang on a sec. That's part of your trick, right?' Michael: 'No.' Gob: 'That's not my trick, Michael.'
jokes.setup: Rita is apparently walking on water. Gob looks for a magic explanation.
jokes.punchline: Gob — the magician who has been trying to do tricks all episode — is confronted with something he didn't do and cannot explain. 'That's not my trick, Michael' has genuine existential weight for Gob.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. Gob denying responsibility for what looks like his best trick ever — while clearly wishing it were his — is perfect character comedy. The economy is superb: 'That's not my trick, Michael' in four words conveys Gob's frustration, wonder, and professional jealousy. The callback to all his failed tricks gives it maximum earned quality. This is what Gob's character is for.

jokes.text: On the Next Arrested Development: Gob's illusion at the wedding — 'Why am I not going under water? Dear God, why am I not going under water?'
jokes.setup: The 'On the Next' teaser shows Gob attempting an underwater illusion at a subsequent wedding.
jokes.punchline: Gob screaming 'Why am I not going under water? Dear God, why am I not going under water?' — implying he desperately WANTS to be submerged, perhaps as an escape from the consequences of his trick.
jokes.explanation: 'Dear God, why am I not going under water?' is a great closer — the inversion of normal survival instinct (wanting to be underwater) applied to Gob's magic desperation. Teases consequences for the next episode. Good button on a strong episode.

tmdb_overview: After Michael announces he's marrying Rita, George Sr. worries that she's after the family's money. But when Rita's Uncle Trevor tries to stop the wedding by revealing some surprising information about Rita, Michael must decide if he wants to walk down the aisle.
insight: Ocean Walker escalates absurdist chaos to 81.9 with 2.87 jokes per minute across 74 gags.
